reasoning: C wins because its Tensile Sling attacks a primitive no other answer touches, the direction force takes against gravity (hanging from cables/ceiling, routing load up-and-out rather than down through legs), and its set spans the widest axes (physics, object-status, rest, sociality, machine agency) with vivid craft and a genuinely bold Reading Chair that can override and evict its occupant. B is a strong second: the Tithe is the single most non-obvious idea in the field, the only one to attack the economic and honesty primitive (a free chair whose real product is your body's telemetry), and B explicitly cross-checks for divergence. D ranks third on real vividness and two fresh ideas, the Negotiated Chair as a two-body coupled equilibrium and the Last Chair as a single-use plant-it-back object, though several of its other moves (grown organism, anti-rest) echo common tropes. A is last despite the cleanest prose because its five lean on the most familiar reinvention beats shared across the others (air seat, unrestful seat, living-dying chair) without a singular surprising idea, and its Metabolic and Commons concepts overlap conceptually rather than opening distinct territory.
